How Trump Took the U.S. to War With Iran
Benjamin Netanyahu in the Situation Room. A pessimistic intelligence assessment. And a president with almost boundless trust in his own instincts.
Al Drago for The New York Times

Benjamin Netanyahu made his war pitch from inside the Situation Room — a setting rarely used for in-person foreign leader meetings. The audience was President Trump and his inner circle. It proved to be a fateful meeting, as my colleague Jonathan Swan and I show in new reporting.
Netanyahu outlined scenarios for regime change that C.I.A. Director John Ratcliffe the next day privately described as “farcical” and Secretary of State Marco Rubio called “bullshit.”

JD Vance was the only person in Trump’s inner circle who actively tried to stop the march to war. He warned the president and others on the senior team it would shatter Trump’s coalition, cause regional chaos, dangerously deplete U.S. munitions and spike oil prices.

Several top advisers had real doubts — but not enough to lean in hard. Rubio was ambivalent. The Joint Chiefs chairman Dan Caine flagged risks but said it was not his role to take a position. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth was the most enthusiastic in the inner circle.

Trump believed a war with Iran would be quick and decisive — like his operation to capture Maduro in Venezuela. When aides warned about oil disruptions and weapons shortages, he waved them off. Asked by Tucker Carlson how he could be so sure everything would be OK, Trump replied: “Because it always is.”

This was a gut decision. One underestimated thing about Trump is that he is more hawkish than his advisers on Iran. Many in the “America First” movement ignored Trump’s record of aggressive policy toward Iran. And his feelings only deepened after his first term when Iran plotted to kill him.

Trump’s second-term advisers see their primary jobs as executing on his desires and decisions rather than to argue for their own views. They mostly regard him as a great man of history who often sees things others don’t. This term, for Trump, almost nothing stands between the instinct and the act.
Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
